bull.b: The company’s minimal debt and ~0.01 debt-to-equity materially reduce interest and refinancing pressure, preserving financing optionality. Over 2–6 months this lowers bankruptcy risk and gives management time to pursue financing or partnerships without heavy fixed-charge constraints.

Skye’s focus on cannabinoid-based therapeutics targeting the endocannabinoid system and CB1 for metabolic disease establishes a differentiated R&D niche. If clinical progress continues, this structural specialization could create durable IP-driven competitive advantages and address large unmet markets.

Expanding authorized shares plus shareholder re-approval of directors and auditor gives management formal flexibility to raise capital, execute strategic transactions, or issue equity for partnerships. Combined with shareholder backing, this supports longer-term funding options if needed.

bear.b: Sustained zero revenue with large TTM losses and roughly -$42M free cash flow implies continued reliance on external financing. Over months this depletes liquidity, constrains R&D runway absent fundraising, and increases execution risk if capital markets tighten or dilution becomes necessary.

A sharp decline in stockholders’ equity signals sustained losses and capital consumption, weakening the balance sheet. Over 2–6 months this limits strategic flexibility, heightens the probability of dilutive financings, and undermines resilience to unexpected shocks or extended clinical timelines.

Transfer to the lower-tier Nasdaq Capital Market and an outstanding minimum bid-price deficiency create structural listing risk and potential governance distractions. If unresolved, these issues can reduce investor access, increase fundraising costs, and limit strategic transactions over the medium term.
